topic: How to write a proper paragraph
title: How to Write a Proper Paragraph – Adults

hunks.text: A PARAGRAPH is a group of sentences that work together to explain one main idea. Think of a paragraph as a mini-essay—it has a clear beginning, middle, and end. The TOPIC SENTENCE is the first sentence, and it tells the reader exactly what the paragraph will be about. Every other sentence in the paragraph should support or explain that topic sentence. When you write a proper paragraph, your reader knows what to expect and can follow your thinking easily.

questions.question: What is the first sentence of a paragraph called?
questions.answer: TOPIC SENTENCE

questions.question: What is the job of the topic sentence?
questions.answer: TO TELL THE READER WHAT THE PARAGRAPH WILL BE ABOUT

questions.question: Name two things the text says about what a paragraph is.
questions.answer: A GROUP OF SENTENCES THAT WORK TOGETHER / HAS A CLEAR BEGINNING, MIDDLE, AND END / EXPLAINS ONE MAIN IDEA / A MINI-ESSAY

questions.question: Imagine opening a book and reading the first sentence of a paragraph—picture yourself thinking about what comes next. What feeling do you get when the first sentence is clear?
questions.answer: UNDERSTANDING / CONFIDENCE / INTEREST / CURIOSITY / CLARITY

hunks.text: The body of your paragraph comes after the topic sentence, and this is where you provide SUPPORTING DETAILS, EXAMPLES, and EVIDENCE. These sentences explain why your topic sentence is true or important. You might use facts from research, personal experiences, or logical reasoning to back up your main idea. Strong supporting sentences make your paragraph believable and interesting. Without good supporting details, your paragraph feels empty and leaves the reader asking, 'So what?'

questions.question: What three types of information can you use in supporting sentences?
questions.answer: SUPPORTING DETAILS / EXAMPLES / EVIDENCE

questions.question: What happens to a paragraph without good supporting details?
questions.answer: IT FEELS EMPTY / IT LEAVES THE READER ASKING 'SO WHAT?'

questions.question: What does the text tell us we might use to back up our main idea?
questions.answer: FACTS FROM RESEARCH / PERSONAL EXPERIENCES / LOGICAL REASONING

questions.question: Think about the last time you tried to convince someone of something. What kind of supporting detail would have helped you most?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

hunks.text: COHERENCE and UNITY are two essential qualities that make a paragraph work well together. Coherence means your ideas flow smoothly from one sentence to the next—your reader never feels lost or confused. Unity means every single sentence belongs to that paragraph and supports the main idea; if a sentence is off-topic, it breaks unity and confuses your reader. You can improve coherence by using TRANSITION WORDS like 'however,' 'for example,' 'in addition,' and 'as a result.' These words act like bridges between your sentences.

questions.question: What does coherence mean in a paragraph?
questions.answer: YOUR IDEAS FLOW SMOOTHLY FROM ONE SENTENCE TO THE NEXT

questions.question: What is the purpose of transition words?
questions.answer: TO ACT LIKE BRIDGES BETWEEN SENTENCES

questions.question: Name two transition words the text gives as examples.
questions.answer: HOWEVER / FOR EXAMPLE / IN ADDITION / AS A RESULT

questions.question: Can you think of another transition word not mentioned in this text?
questions.answer: FURTHERMORE / ADDITIONALLY / MEANWHILE / THEREFORE / CONVERSELY / SIMILARLY

hunks.text: The CLOSING SENTENCE (or CONCLUSION) is the last sentence of your paragraph, and it wraps up your main idea. This sentence reminds the reader what your paragraph was about, or it shows why your topic sentence matters. You do not introduce completely new information in your closing sentence—that confuses your reader. A strong closing sentence might RESTATE your topic in a new way, link back to a larger idea, or leave your reader with something to think about. Think of your closing sentence as the final word that helps your reader feel satisfied.

questions.question: What is the job of the closing sentence?
questions.answer: TO WRAP UP YOUR MAIN IDEA / TO REMIND THE READER WHAT THE PARAGRAPH WAS ABOUT / TO SHOW WHY YOUR TOPIC SENTENCE MATTERS

questions.question: What should you NOT do in your closing sentence?
questions.answer: INTRODUCE COMPLETELY NEW INFORMATION

questions.question: What are three things a strong closing sentence might do, according to the text?
questions.answer: RESTATE YOUR TOPIC IN A NEW WAY / LINK BACK TO A LARGER IDEA / LEAVE YOUR READER WITH SOMETHING TO THINK ABOUT

questions.question: Imagine finishing a paragraph and reading the last sentence aloud to a friend. What would you want them to feel when they hear it?
questions.answer: SATISFIED / COMPLETE / UNDERSTANDING / CLARITY

hunks.text: ORGANIZATION and LOGICAL ORDER are critical to paragraph success. Your sentences should follow a clear pattern—whether that is CHRONOLOGICAL (time order), CAUSE AND EFFECT, ORDER OF IMPORTANCE, or SPATIAL (location-based). If you jump around randomly, your reader loses the thread of your argument. Before you write, take a moment to plan which idea comes first, which comes second, and which comes last. Professional writers outline or use graphic organizers to map out their paragraph structure before they start typing.

questions.question: Name four types of logical order you can use to organize a paragraph.
questions.answer: CHRONOLOGICAL / CAUSE AND EFFECT / ORDER OF IMPORTANCE / SPATIAL

questions.question: What happens if you jump around randomly in your paragraph?
questions.answer: YOUR READER LOSES THE THREAD OF YOUR ARGUMENT

questions.question: What does the text suggest you can do before you start writing?
questions.answer: PLAN WHICH IDEA COMES FIRST / OUTLINE / USE GRAPHIC ORGANIZERS / MAP OUT YOUR PARAGRAPH STRUCTURE

questions.question: Which type of logical order would you use if you were writing about how to bake a cake?
questions.answer: CHRONOLOGICAL

hunks.text: WORD CHOICE and TONE affect how your paragraph sounds to the reader. Your VOCABULARY should match your PURPOSE and AUDIENCE—if you are writing an email to your boss, your tone is formal and professional; if you are texting a friend, your tone is casual and relaxed. Avoid using words you do not fully understand, because that makes your writing sound awkward or dishonest. Instead, use clear, direct words that you know well. Strong paragraphs use ACTIVE VOICE (the subject does the action) rather than passive voice, which makes your writing more powerful and easier to read.

questions.question: What two things affect how a paragraph sounds to the reader?
questions.answer: WORD CHOICE / TONE

questions.question: What does the text recommend you do instead of using words you do not fully understand?
questions.answer: USE CLEAR, DIRECT WORDS THAT YOU KNOW WELL

questions.question: Name two examples of different audiences the text mentions.
questions.answer: YOUR BOSS / A FRIEND

questions.question: Read this aloud: 'The problem was solved by the team.' Now imagine rewriting it in active voice: 'The team solved the problem.' Which version feels stronger and clearer when you say it?
questions.answer: THE TEAM SOLVED THE PROBLEM

hunks.text: EDITING and REVISION are not optional steps—they are essential parts of writing a proper paragraph. Your first draft is rarely your best draft. After you write, read your paragraph aloud to catch awkward phrasing, REPETITION, or unclear sentences. Ask yourself: Does every sentence belong here? Is my topic sentence clear? Do I have enough supporting details? Are my transitions smooth? Professional writers revise their work multiple times before it is ready to share. Do not be embarrassed to cross out sentences or rewrite them—that is exactly what real writers do.

questions.question: Why is editing and revision essential to writing a proper paragraph?
questions.answer: YOUR FIRST DRAFT IS RARELY YOUR BEST DRAFT

questions.question: What are three questions the text suggests you ask yourself while revising?
questions.answer: DOES EVERY SENTENCE BELONG HERE? / IS MY TOPIC SENTENCE CLEAR? / DO I HAVE ENOUGH SUPPORTING DETAILS? / ARE MY TRANSITIONS SMOOTH?

questions.question: What should you do if you notice awkward phrasing when reading your paragraph aloud?
questions.answer: CROSS OUT SENTENCES OR REWRITE THEM

questions.question: What is the hardest part of revising your own writing, in your opinion?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

hunks.text: Now that you understand the structure of a proper paragraph, practice is the key to getting better. Write paragraphs about topics you care about—your job, your family, your goals, or your interests. Remember: a proper paragraph has a clear TOPIC SENTENCE, strong SUPPORTING DETAILS, smooth TRANSITIONS, and a satisfying CLOSING SENTENCE. Your paragraph should have COHERENCE and UNITY, with sentences in LOGICAL ORDER. Do not aim for perfection on your first try; instead, focus on improving with each paragraph you write. The more you practice these skills, the more natural and confident your writing will become.

questions.question: Name four elements of a proper paragraph mentioned in this hunk.
questions.answer: CLEAR TOPIC SENTENCE / STRONG SUPPORTING DETAILS / SMOOTH TRANSITIONS / SATISFYING CLOSING SENTENCE

questions.question: What is the key to getting better at writing paragraphs?
questions.answer: PRACTICE

questions.question: What is one topic you care about that you could write a paragraph about?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

questions.question: After learning about proper paragraphs, what part of writing feels easiest to you now?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

citations: Strunk, W., & White, E.B. (2000). The Elements of Style (4th ed.). Longman.
Williams, J.M., & Bizup, J. (2017). Style: Lessons in Clarity and Grace (12th ed.). Pearson.
